description_long: This object is a vintage tabletop radio receiver with a polished wooden cabinet featuring a rich, natural wood grain pattern. The front panel displays a rectangular glass window with a frequency indicator and a pair of large, round tuning knobs at the lower corners. Below the glass window, there is a horizontal speaker grille with two black horizontal bars, likely made of plastic or Bakelite, covering a fabric mesh. The sides and top are smoothly curved, showcasing the craftsmanship of the woodwork, while the back is open, revealing vacuum tubes, wiring, and other internal electronic components typical of mid-20th-century radios. The overall design is both functional and decorative, reflecting the style of classic radios from the 1930s to 1950s. This radio would be a striking feature in an urban setting, such as a retro-themed café, antique shop, or as a collectible in a residential garage or living room.
description: A vintage wooden tabletop radio receiver with a glass frequency display, large tuning knobs, and a horizontal speaker grille, featuring exposed vacuum tubes at the back. The polished wood cabinet and classic design evoke mid-20th-century style.
description_view_0: Front view showing the glass frequency display, two large tuning knobs, and the horizontal speaker grille with black bars.
description_view_1: Left side view highlighting the smooth, polished wood surface and rounded edges, with one tuning knob visible.
description_view_2: Rear view exposing the internal components, including vacuum tubes, wiring, and metal chassis, with the back panel removed.
description_view_3: Right side view similar to the left, showing the continuous wood grain and the other tuning knob.

license_info.creator_display_name: Museum of Engineering and Technology, Krakow
